1: שְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל (shĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) — "Hear, O Israel." Moses opens with the same summons that frames the great commandment (Deuteronomy 6:4) and Israel's foundational confession. Shema (H8085) is more than hearing; it implies attentive obedience — "hear and do." The call to all of Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl) stresses the covenant community as a whole, not merely its leaders.
2: כָּרַת (kārat, H3772) — "cut a covenant": the standard Hebrew idiom for covenant-making, rooted in the sacrificial rite of cutting animals (Genesis 15:10). The covenant was made at Horeb (the Deuteronomic name for Sinai), tying the legal content of Deuteronomy 5–26 to the historical event of the Exodus generation's encounter with YHWH.
3: The claim "not with our fathers but with us" resists covenant-as-inheritance in a passive sense. Every generation stands directly before YHWH as the covenant party. Anachnu (we) is emphatic: the covenant is not a legal artifact passed down but a living relation each generation must own. This logic drives Moses's renewal-preaching in Deuteronomy as a whole.
4: פָּנִים בְּפָנִים (pānîm bĕpānîm) — "face to face": the most direct possible mode of divine communication. The same phrase describes Moses's unique prophetic access (Deuteronomy 34:10; Numbers 12:8). The people's experience at Sinai/Horeb — hearing the divine voice from fire — was not mediated by human intermediary at that moment; the mediation (v5) came because of their fear afterward.
5: עָמַד בֵּין (ʿāmad bên) — "I stood between": Moses's intercessory position is both spatial (between the people and the divine fire) and covenantal (mediating word). The same verb root ʿmd describes the priest standing before YHWH to minister. Moses's role combines prophetic (declaring the word), priestly (standing between), and quasi-judicial (bringing YHWH's verdict to the people) functions.
6: The preamble to the Decalogue — "I am YHWH your God who brought you out" — precedes all commands. This is the standard suzerainty-treaty order: the great king identifies himself and cites his past benefactions before issuing stipulations. The commands that follow are responses to prior grace, not conditions for earning it. Bêt ʿăbādîm ("house of slavery") is Israel's pre-covenant identity; the commands define who the redeemed are now.
7: עַל פָּנַי (ʿal pānāy) — traditionally "before me" but literally "against my face" or "to my face." The idiom may mean "in my presence" (no other gods where I am present, i.e., anywhere) or "in addition to me" (no gods as rivals). The spatial sense reinforces that the prohibition is not merely theoretical monotheism but relational exclusivity: the God who redeemed Israel will not share the covenant relationship.
8: פֶּסֶל (pesel, H6459) — a graven/carved image; תְּמוּנָה (tĕmûnāh) — a likeness or form. The prohibition covers both making and possessing images. The scope is comprehensive: heaven above, earth beneath, waters under earth — the three tiers of the ancient cosmology. The concern is not merely idolatry toward foreign gods but any visual representation of YHWH himself (Deuteronomy 4:15-16: "you saw no form").
9: אֵל קַנָּא (ʾēl qannāʾ) — "a jealous God." Qannāʾ (H7067) is used exclusively of YHWH in the OT and denotes the exclusive claim of the covenant relationship — not petty envy but the righteous intolerance of covenant violation. The "visiting iniquity of fathers on children" clause reflects corporate solidarity: covenant faithfulness (or unfaithfulness) has generational consequences. The contrast in v10 — "steadfast love to thousands" — shows the asymmetry: punishment extends to 3-4 generations; ḥesed extends to thousands.
10: חֶסֶד (ḥesed, H2617) — steadfast love, covenantal loyalty. The contrast between the third-to-fourth generation of v9 and "thousands" here is deliberately asymmetric: divine judgment is limited and proportional; divine ḥesed is extensive and generous. The recipients are those who "love me and keep my commandments" — the covenant-loyal community. Ḥesed is not unconditional sentimentality but the faithfulness of a covenant partner who rewards the faithful.
11: לַשָּׁוְא (laššāwĕʾ, H7723) — "for emptiness/worthlessness/falsehood." The prohibition covers not only profanity (using YHWH's name as a curse or oath-word) but invoking the divine name for empty, false, or deceptive purposes — swearing falsely, invoking YHWH to guarantee a lie, or treating the divine name as a magical formula. Shāwĕʾ has the semantic range of "vain," "worthless," "false" — the name of the living God is not to be wielded for worthless ends.
12: שָׁמוֹר (shāmôr) — "observe, guard." In Exodus 20:8, the command is zākor ("remember") the Sabbath. Deuteronomy substitutes "observe/guard" (shāmôr) — not a contradiction but a complementary nuance: Exodus ties Sabbath to creation-memory (God rested); Deuteronomy ties it to liberation-memory and the ethic of rest for all, including slaves (v14-15).
13: Six days of labor (ʿāsîtā kol-mĕlākĕtekā) are affirmed, not merely permitted: work is part of the created order and covenant faithfulness. The Sabbath is the seventh-day cessation from the six-day norm, not a replacement for it.
14: The Deuteronomic Sabbath extends rest explicitly to the ben-amatkā (your male slave) and bat-amatkā (female slave), and to the sojourner (gēr) within the gates. Exodus 20 lists the same beneficiaries but in a more compressed form. The humanitarian motivation — "so that your slave may rest as you do" — is characteristically Deuteronomic, grounding law in empathy rooted in Israel's own experience of servitude.
15: זָכַרְתָּ כִּי עֶבֶד הָיִיתָ (zākarttā kî ʿebed hāyîtā) — "remember that you were a slave." The Sabbath in Deuteronomy is grounded in the Exodus, not creation (Exodus 20:11). Memory of servitude becomes the ethical foundation for extending rest to those who serve. The "mighty hand and outstretched arm" (yād ḥăzāqāh ûzĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyāh) is a fixed formulaic pair for the Exodus event — it appears frequently in Deuteronomy as the experiential basis for covenant obligation.
16: כַּבֵּד אֶת אָבִיךָ וְאֶת אִמֶּךָ (kabbēd ʾet-ʾābîkā wĕʾet-ʾimmekā): "Honor your father and your mother." Kabbēd (H3513, piel of כָּבַד) means to make heavy/weighty, i.e., to treat as significant and authoritative — not merely affection but social recognition of parental authority. The attached promise — "that your days may be long and that it may go well with you" — is the first commandment with a promise (Ephesians 6:2-3), and Paul cites it as still applicable to NT believers under a new framework.
17: לֹא תִרְצָח (lōʾ tirṣaḥ): The verb rāṣaḥ (H7523) is not the general term for killing (hārag or mût) but the specific term for unlawful homicide — murder, manslaughter requiring legal process (Numbers 35:16-28). It does not prohibit all killing (capital punishment and holy war are commanded elsewhere); it prohibits the taking of human life outside the covenant community's authorized frameworks.
18: לֹא תִנְאָף (lōʾ tinʾāp): nāʾap (H5003) — sexual betrayal of the covenant. The sexual act here is covenantally framed: marriage is the covenant between husband and wife; adultery violates both the human covenant and Israel's self-understanding as YHWH's covenant-spouse. The prophets use adultery as the primary image for Israel's idolatry (Hosea, Ezekiel) — the two transgressions share the same covenantal structure.
19: לֹא תִגְנֹב (lōʾ tignōb): gānab (H1589) — to steal, but also to deceive or act covertly. Rabbinic tradition reads this commandment as prohibiting kidnapping (carrying a person off to slavery) rather than property theft, since property theft has its own specific legislation. On either reading, it protects the physical and social integrity of the covenant neighbor.
20: עֵד שָׁוְא (ʿēd shāwĕʾ) — "a worthless/false witness." Deuteronomy uses shāwĕʾ (worthless/false) in place of Exodus 20:16's šeqer (lying). The distinction may be slight, but shāwĕʾ adds the nuance of testimony that is empty or groundless, not just deliberately false. Legal testimony in the covenant community carries divine weight; groundless accusation corrupts justice at its foundation.
21: Deuteronomy uses two different verbs in v21 where Exodus 20:17 uses one. For coveting a neighbor's wife: חָמַד (ḥāmad, H2530) — to delight in, to desire with pleasure. For coveting a neighbor's house, field, etc.: תִּתְאַוֶּה (titʾawweh, from אָוָה H183) — to wish for, to crave. The choice of two different verbs may distinguish relational desire (for a person) from acquisitive desire (for property), or may simply be stylistic variation. In either case, both forms of covetousness are prohibited as violations of the neighbor's whole life and estate.
22: קוֹל גָּדוֹל (qôl gādôl) — "a great voice": the divine voice at Sinai/Horeb is a sustained, overwhelming auditory experience. Welōʾ yāsāp — "he did not add more," implying these ten words are the complete, sealed direct divine speech. The Decalogue is uniquely distinguished: written on stone by God, spoken directly to the whole assembly without human mediation. Everything else in the law came through Moses.
23: The elders and tribal heads (rāʾšê šibṭêkem) approach Moses — the leaders acting for the people as a whole. The "darkness" (ḥōšek) and "cloud" (ʿānān) are the typical theophanic veil: YHWH's presence manifests in phenomena that conceal as they reveal, protecting the people from the unmediated divine glory.
24: כְּבֹדוֹ וְאֶת גָּדְלוֹ (kĕbōdô wĕʾet-godlô) — "his glory and greatness." Kābôd (H3519) is the weightiness, radiance, manifest importance of YHWH's presence; gādal (H1431) is the extensiveness of his power and works. The people's admission — "we have heard his voice from the fire" — confirms the unique standing of the Sinaitic theophany in Israel's history.
25: The people's fear of death if they continue to hear YHWH's voice directly becomes the occasion for Mosaic mediation. "This great fire will consume us" — the divine holiness is lethal to those without adequate standing before it. This is not pusillanimity but accurate anthropological assessment: the creature cannot sustain direct confrontation with uncreated holiness without a mediating structure.
26: אֱלֹהִים חַיִּים (ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm) — "the living God": this title distinguishes YHWH from dead idols that cannot speak. The question "who among all flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from fire and lived?" marks the Sinai theophany as existentially unique. The answer is: only Israel, and they survived only through YHWH's mercy.
27: דַּבֵּר אַתָּה (dabbēr ʾattāh) — "you speak to us." The people's delegation of hearing to Moses is the foundation of the prophetic institution. Moses as the covenant mediator becomes the pattern for all subsequent prophets, and ultimately for the prophet-like-Moses still to come (Deuteronomy 18:15). The people's request is honored: YHWH approves it (v28) and establishes Moses as the permanent mouthpiece.
28: YHWH's response — "they are right in all they have spoken" — validates the people's instinct. Their fear is not faithlessness; their delegation to Moses is appropriate. The divine approval of the mediatorial structure is significant: YHWH himself institutes the pattern of indirect revelation through a human spokesperson, which becomes the constitutive structure of the prophetic office.
29: מִי יִתֵּן (mî yittēn) — the idiom of wishing: "O that they had such a heart as this always." The divine wish expresses genuine pathos: YHWH desires the people's sustained covenantal fidelity but recognizes they do not have the heart for it. This verse is the OT's sharpest pre-Jeremiah expression of the new covenant problem — external law without transformed heart is structurally insufficient. Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26 will promise the solution: a new heart.
30: The command "return to your tents" disperses the assembly back to domestic life after the theophanic encounter. The juxtaposition with v31 ("but as for you, stand here with me") underscores Moses's unique station: Israel returns to ordinary life; Moses remains in the divine presence to receive the full law.
31: אֶת כָּל הַמִּצְוָה (ʾet kol-hammitsĕwāh) — "the whole commandment." The singular mitswāh followed by the plural ḥuqqîm ûmishpāṭîm (statutes and rules) reflects Deuteronomy's structure: the one commandment (covenant loyalty) expressed in many stipulations. The law is a unity with a variegated expression.
32: לֹא תְסוּרוּ יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול (lōʾ tāsûrû yāmîn ûśmōʾl) — "do not turn aside to the right or to the left." This formula for covenant faithfulness — walking the exact path without deviation — recurs throughout Deuteronomy and the Deuteronomistic History. It becomes the criterion for evaluating the kings of Israel: did they walk in the ways of David, or did they turn aside?
33: The goal of covenant obedience is life: lĕmaʿan tiḥyûn ("so that you may live"). Deuteronomy is consistently shaped by the life-or-death stakes of covenant keeping. The choice will be formalized in Deuteronomy 30:15-20 as the covenant's climax: life and good, or death and evil. Obedience is not the condition for earning life but the path within which covenant life is sustained.
